map.subtitle: Unit 2: Securing Spaces   •   8 days   •   LO 2.1.A through 2.1.G
map.intro: Everything for this lesson lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Days 1–8 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, cold-call prompts, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with QOTD warm-ups, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Days 1–8 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: Social Engineering Tactic Identifier and Exercise 2: Phishing Advisor (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — Hacking the Human (3-part: Opening Day 1, Mid-lesson Day 2, Closing Day 5)
Quiz (16 questions, student + answer KEY — canonical in the Day-1 master JSON, spanning all of 2.1.A–G)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the daily instruction you project and teach from (Day 1 social engineering, Day 2 adversaries, Day 3 attack phases, Day 4 risk assessment, Days 5–7 managing risk and controls and defense-in-depth, Day 8 quiz).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.

Naming: the CED topic is 'Cyber Foundations'; there is no unit scenario for this topic, so each day's examples — including the Day-1 help-desk call (Scenario 2A in this course's materials) — are built fresh to teach the EKs. The unit hub lists this lesson as 'Cyber Foundations.' All refer to this lesson.

exercise1.title: Social Engineering Tactic Identifier
exercise1.intro: You are a security-awareness analyst triaging reported messages and calls. Four incidents just landed in your queue. For each one you will name the believable cover story, identify which named social engineering tactics are in play, and classify whether the lever is one of pressure or one of trust. Part A works the incident table; Part B extends the same skills. Everything maps to CB Learning Objective 2.1.A and connects forward to 2.1.B.
exercise1.narrative: Four employees forwarded suspicious contacts to your awareness inbox this week. Your intake form logs the channel, the cover story the sender used, the pressure or trust signals in the message, and what the sender wanted the target to do. Read the table row by row, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: anchor your first instinct, find the pretext that opens the contact (EK 2.1.A.2), then name every additional named tactic and cite each EK.

columns: Pressure / trust signals in the message
rows: Caller says he is from the IT help desk running a required security audit.
Demands the password in the next ten minutes or access is cut off and the manager is notified.
Read your account password aloud to the caller.
Sender claims to be Dana, the new VP of Finance, texting from a personal number before a flight.
Needs the vendor banking file in five minutes or the payment deadline is missed.
Send the confidential vendor banking file to the number.
Caller says he is the new floor supervisor doing a routine password reset for everyone.
Says 'everyone else on your floor already reset through this link today — you're the only one who hasn't.'
Reset your password through the link the caller provides.
Email says it is from a vendor offering a one-time licensing deal for the team.
Only three seats remain and the offer closes at the end of the day — act now or lose it.
Enter a company purchasing-card number on the linked order page.

exercise1.scenarioNote: Contacts adapted from the Day 1 bell ringer, Stop-and-Think, and quiz items; tactic names and EKs follow the College Board AP Cybersecurity Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2026). The help-desk call is this course's Scenario 2A teaching example.
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: of the four contacts, which one stacks the MOST named tactics, and what is the single detail that gives that away?

partA.q: Contact 1 (the help-desk call) stacks four named tactics at once. Name the pretext, then name every additional tactic with its EK, quoting the exact phrase in the row that proves each one.
partA.answer: Pretext (EK 2.1.A.2): 'from the IT help desk running a required security audit' — the believable reason to make contact. Authority (EK 2.1.A.3): posing as IT / an official conducting a 'required' audit impersonates someone with power over the target. Urgency (EK 2.1.A.8): 'in the next ten minutes' is a deadline that forces quick action before the target can verify. Intimidation (EK 2.1.A.4): 'or access is cut off and the manager is notified' states a negative consequence to frighten the target into compliance. Full credit names all four with EKs; naming only the first tactic is the planted trap, because exam scenarios stack levers.

partA.q: Contact 2 (the 'VP of Finance' text) is most clearly two tactics. Name them with EKs, and explain why this counts as social engineering even though it arrived by text rather than email.
partA.answer: Authority (EK 2.1.A.3): the sender claims to be the VP of Finance, someone with power over the target, so refusing feels insubordinate. Urgency (EK 2.1.A.8): 'in five minutes or we miss the payment deadline' is a deadline forcing quick action with no time to verify. It is still social engineering because EK 2.1.A.1 defines the attack by the psychological manipulation of a person, not by the delivery channel — text, email, or call are all just the medium.

partA.q: Contact 3 (the 'floor supervisor' call) leans on one tactic that is easy to confuse with intimidation. Name the correct tactic with its EK, and explain the one detail that rules out intimidation.
partA.answer: The tactic is consensus (EK 2.1.A.5): 'everyone else on your floor already reset … you're the only one who hasn't' creates social pressure by making the target believe the whole group has already done the desired action, so the target follows the crowd. It is NOT intimidation (EK 2.1.A.4) because no negative consequence is stated — there is no threat of lost access or a report; the lever is the fear of being the odd one out, not fear of punishment.

partA.q: Contact 4 (the vendor 'licensing deal' email) is built on a single dominant tactic. Name it with its EK, and contrast a tactic of pressure with a tactic of trust using this row plus Contact 1 as your two examples.
partA.answer: The dominant tactic in Contact 4 is scarcity (EK 2.1.A.6): 'only three seats remain' and 'the offer closes at the end of the day' manufacture limited availability so the target acts before the supposed opportunity disappears. Pressure vs. trust: scarcity and the urgency/intimidation in Contact 1 are tactics that push the target by raising fear of loss or consequence; trust tactics such as consensus (A.5) and familiarity (A.7) instead lower the target's guard. Scarcity is sometimes grouped with the trust set on the CED, but functionally it pressures the target to act fast — full credit explains the push-vs-lower-guard distinction.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: Across all four contacts, the pretext came first. Using EK 2.1.A.1 and EK 2.1.A.2, explain why the pretext is the 'opening move' and why every other tactic depends on it.
partB.answer: EK 2.1.A.1 says social engineering manipulates a person into a desired action, and EK 2.1.A.2 defines pretexting as a believable reason to make contact. The pretext is the opening move because it makes the contact plausible enough that the target stays engaged — picks up the call, opens the email — instead of dismissing it. Once the cover story is accepted, authority, urgency, consensus, and the rest have a stage to operate on; without a believable pretext, the target hangs up and no other lever ever gets pulled.

partB.q: Three of the four contacts could have been stopped by the same one-step defense. Name that defense and explain why it neutralizes pressure tactics like urgency (EK 2.1.A.8) and intimidation (EK 2.1.A.4) in particular.
partB.answer: The defense is to slow down and verify through a known, independent channel — call the real IT desk, the real VP, or the real vendor using a number you already trust. It specifically neutralizes urgency (EK 2.1.A.8) and intimidation (EK 2.1.A.4) because both tactics work by removing the time and the calm needed to check: urgency manufactures a deadline and intimidation manufactures fear, and independently verifying restores exactly the pause both tactics try to steal.

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: Connect this lesson forward to LO 2.1.B. Which adversary type (with its EK) would most plausibly run the Contact 1 help-desk call as a step toward a larger attack, and why?
partB.answer: Most plausibly a transnational criminal organization (EK 2.1.B.5) seeking financial gain, using the harvested password as initial access toward ransomware; an insider adversary (EK 2.1.B.3) is also defensible if the caller already has internal knowledge. Credit any named adversary whose motivation (recognition, cause, revenge, disruption, or money) is tied logically to the goal of the social engineering call.

exercise2.title: Phishing Advisor
exercise2.subtitle: 3 messages - Three-Step Method
exercise2.intro: You are a security-awareness advisor. Three messages were escalated to you this week. Message 1 is the Day-1 help-desk call (Scenario 2A). For each, run the tactic check (which named tactics from EK 2.1.A.2–A.8 are present), state the mechanism — why the stack works and the single fix that defeats all of it — and name the most effective response. Advanced students also name the enrichment delivery sub-type. Vague 'be careful' advice scores zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): tactic check + mechanism + best response for all 3 messages. Enrichment (optional): also name the phishing delivery sub-type.
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every message
method: Before analyzing, write your gut read of who the sender claims to be and what they want. Anchoring your own answer first keeps a convincing pretext from sweeping you along.

Find the pretext (A.2) that opens the contact, then mark each named tactic — authority (A.3), intimidation (A.4), consensus (A.5), scarcity (A.6), familiarity (A.7), urgency (A.8) — as PRESENT or ABSENT with the exact phrase as evidence.

Explain why the stacked levers reinforce each other (EK 2.1.A.1), then name the single most effective response: slow down and verify through a known, independent channel rather than the channel the sender supplied.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment delivery sub-types (NOT required by the CED for Topic 2.1): Phishing = a deceptive message, usually email, casting wide. Spear phishing = a targeted message using gathered personal detail. Vishing = voice/phone phishing (a phone-call pretext). Smishing = SMS/text phishing. Whaling = spear phishing aimed at a high-value executive. The named tactics in EK 2.1.A.2–A.8 are what you must identify; the delivery sub-type is enrichment only.

scenarios.text: Scenario 2A — The Help-Desk Call. An employee answers a phone call. The caller says he is from the IT help desk, that the account was flagged in a required security audit, and that he needs the password in the next ten minutes or access will be cut off and the manager notified. He already knows the employee's name and department.

key.signs: Pretext (EK 2.1.A.2): 'from the IT help desk running a required security audit.' Authority PRESENT (EK 2.1.A.3): impersonating IT / an official with power. Urgency PRESENT (EK 2.1.A.8): 'in the next ten minutes.' Intimidation PRESENT (EK 2.1.A.4): 'or access is cut off and your manager is notified.' Knowing the name and department adds a touch of familiarity (EK 2.1.A.7). Consensus and scarcity ABSENT.
key.mechanism: The pretext keeps the target on the line; authority makes refusing feel insubordinate; urgency strips away the time to verify; intimidation raises the cost of saying no. Each lever covers the previous one's weakness. Best response: refuse to give the password, hang up, and call the real IT help desk using a number from the company directory — verifying through a known, independent channel defeats all four levers at once. Legitimate IT never asks for your password.
key.subtype: Vishing — voice/phone phishing (a phone-call pretext). Because the caller used the employee's real name and department, it edges toward spear phishing / targeted vishing.

scenarios.text: An employee gets a text from an unknown number: 'This is Dana, your new VP of Finance — I'm about to board a flight and need the vendor banking file in the next five minutes or we miss the payment deadline. Don't loop anyone else in.' The employee has never met Dana.

key.signs: Pretext (EK 2.1.A.2): 'your new VP of Finance' texting before a flight. Authority PRESENT (EK 2.1.A.3): claims a senior title with power over the target. Urgency PRESENT (EK 2.1.A.8): 'in the next five minutes or we miss the payment deadline.' The 'don't loop anyone else in' isolates the target — arguably intimidation/familiarity pressure (EK 2.1.A.4 / A.7). Consensus and scarcity ABSENT.
key.mechanism: Authority plus urgency plus isolation is a classic business-email-compromise stack: the title pressures compliance, the deadline removes verification time, and 'don't loop anyone in' blocks the one check that would expose it. Best response: do NOT send the file; verify Dana's identity and request through a known channel — the corporate directory or a known-good number — never by replying to the unknown number. A brand-new VP demanding a confidential file by text is the red flag.
key.subtype: Smishing (SMS/text phishing); because it targets a specific role for a specific file and impersonates an executive, it is also whaling-style spear phishing.

scenarios.text: An email arrives from 'IT-Security' addressed to the whole department: 'Scheduled maintenance is complete. Everyone has already re-verified their mailbox through the secure portal below; you are one of the last few who has not. The verification window closes tonight.' The link goes to a page that asks for the network password.

key.signs: Pretext (EK 2.1.A.2): 'IT-Security … scheduled maintenance is complete.' Consensus PRESENT (EK 2.1.A.5): 'everyone has already re-verified … you are one of the last few.' Scarcity/urgency PRESENT (EK 2.1.A.6 / A.8): 'the verification window closes tonight.' Authority leans in via the 'IT-Security' sender (EK 2.1.A.3). Intimidation and overt familiarity ABSENT.
key.mechanism: This stack lowers the guard rather than raising fear: consensus says the crowd already complied so the target follows, while the closing window adds just enough time pressure to discourage checking. Best response: do not click the link; report the email to IT and verify any real mailbox action through the official IT portal or help desk you already use — credentials are harvested at the linked page, so verifying out-of-band before clicking is the only fix. A legitimate IT notice never collects your password through an emailed link.
key.subtype: Phishing (broad, department-wide email blast); not targeted enough to be spear phishing because it is addressed to the whole department rather than one named person.

discussion.title: Hacking the Human
discussion.minutes: ~25 min total - 3 parts - Opening (Day 1) + Mid-lesson (Day 2) + Closing (Day 5)
discussion.overview: Cyberattacks rarely begin with code — they begin with a person who trusts the wrong message. Across three short windows in this lesson, your group will connect the seven named social engineering tactics to real life, debate who is actually on the other end of these attacks and how their motivation shapes the con, and decide how an organization should manage a risk it can never drive to zero. Throughout, keep asking the question that defeats every tactic: what would slowing down to verify have changed?

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. The help-desk caller never wrote a line of code — he just sounded official, knew your name, and put you on a clock. Of the seven named tactics (pretexting, authority, intimidation, consensus, scarcity, familiarity, urgency), which do you think is hardest for a trained employee to resist in the moment, and why?

Question 2. Whose job is it to stop a social engineering attack — the employee who answers the call, or the organization that should have trained and protected them? Defend your split with one concrete example.
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 2, ~8 min, right after the adversary-types debrief
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - The con behind the call  [EK 2.1.A.1 + EK 2.1.B]
items.text: Yesterday you named the tactics in the help-desk call; today you met the five adversary types. Which adversary would most plausibly run that exact call, and how does their motivation (recognition, cause, revenge, disruption, or financial gain) change what they do with the password once they get it? Agree on the single best one-sentence answer your group can write.

items.label: Task B - Why the insider is different  [EK 2.1.B.3]
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: an insider already has legitimate credentials, so they may not need any social engineering at all. Does that make insiders MORE dangerous than the external caller, or just dangerous in a different way? Record your group's verdict and one reason.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 5, ~9 min, after the managing-risk lesson
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: Living with risk you can't eliminate
items.text: An organization can train every employee and still get one person who falls for the help-desk call — absolute security is unattainable (EK 2.1.E.5). Using the four options for managing risk (avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept — EK 2.1.E.1), design a realistic plan for social engineering risk: which option does the bulk of the work here, why can't this risk simply be avoided, and where does residual risk land? Tie at least one control to defense-in-depth (EK 2.1.G).
Our group's verdict (primary risk strategy + one-sentence justification):
